sections.about: This metric reports workers, or their family members, rehabilitated and placed in suitable employment after high-consequence injury, ill-health or fatalities. It is classified as a Principle 3 Leadership Indicator. A higher value indicates better performance, but it must be read alongside affected-worker volumes.
sections.performanceAndDrivers: Tata increased worker/family rehabilitation from 4 in FY23 to 9 in FY24 and 19 in FY25, the strongest remedial-support trend in the peer set. SAIL improved to 7 in FY25. Jindal moved from 3 in FY23 to 0 in FY24 and FY25, consistent with zero affected workers in FY24-FY25. JSW remained at 0. Tata’s stronger number reflects social protection mechanisms and contractor-worker support architecture, but it also reflects Tata’s higher severe worker incident base. Tata’s FY24 disclosure describes multiple family support, benefit and accident-related schemes, including support for non-permanent workers through the Suraksha Scheme.
sections.tataPositioning: In FY2024-25, Tata ranked 1st with 19 rehabilitated workers/family members. The closest peer was SAIL at 7, which was 63.2% behind Tata. Tata’s rank stayed 1st and the absolute support count improved, but the management interpretation should pair this with Tata’s 24 affected workers in FY25.
sections.comparabilityNotes: This is an absolute remedial-action count, not a rehabilitation rate. Higher values can indicate stronger support, but can also be driven by more severe incidents. Interpret with Total Number of Affected Workers.
